# рҹҢҹ Z-Lang: мӢӨмӢңк°„ мӢңмҠӨн…ңмқ„ мң„н•ң н”„лЎңк·ёлһҳл°Қ м–ём–ҙ

![Status](https://img.shields.io/badge/Status-Development-blue)
![Version](https://img.shields.io/badge/Version-0.1--Dev-green)
![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-MIT-orange)

## мҶҢк°ң (Introduction)

**Z-Lang**мқҖ LLVMмқ„ кё°л°ҳмңјлЎң м„Өкі„лҗң **мғҲлЎңмҡҙ н”„лЎңк·ёлһҳл°Қ м–ём–ҙ**мһ…лӢҲлӢӨ.

мһҗлҸҷм°Ё, мқҳлЈҢкё°кё°, н•ӯкіөмҡ°мЈј л“ұ **мӢӨмӢңк°„ мӢңмҠӨн…ңмқҙ мҡ”кө¬лҗҳлҠ” л¶„м•ј**м—җ нҠ№нҷ”лҗҳм–ҙ мһҲмҠөлӢҲлӢӨ.

```
нҠ№м§•:
  вң… Zero-Latency (GC м—ҶмқҢ)
  вң… Type Safe (м»ҙнҢҢмқј нғҖмһ„ кІҖмҰқ)
  вң… Memory Safe (Ownership + Lifetime)
  вң… Real-Time Guarantees (WCET л¶„м„қ)
  вң… Multi-Platform (x86-64, ARM, RISC-V)
```

---

## рҹҺҜ н”„лЎңм қнҠё лӘ©н‘ң

### лӢЁкё° (8мЈј)
- вң… мҷ„м „н•ң м»ҙнҢҢмқјлҹ¬ кө¬нҳ„ (Lexer вҶ’ Codegen)
- вң… LLVM IR мғқм„ұ
- вң… н‘ңмӨҖ лқјмқҙлёҢлҹ¬лҰ¬ м ңкіө
- вң… н•ҷмҲ  л…јл¬ё мһ‘м„ұ

### мһҘкё°
- рҹҺҜ мһҗмңЁмЈјн–үм°Ё мҡҙмҳҒмІҙм ң кё°ліё м–ём–ҙ
- рҹҺҜ мқҳлЈҢкё°кё° мӢӨмӢңк°„ м ңм–ҙ м–ём–ҙ
- рҹҺҜ н•ӯкіөмҡ°мЈј м•Ҳм „-critical мӢңмҠӨн…ң м–ём–ҙ
- рҹҺҜ мҳӨн”ҲмҶҢмҠӨ м»Өл®ӨлӢҲнӢ° мЈјлҸ„

---

## рҹҡҖ л№ лҘё мӢңмһ‘ (Quick Start)

### мӮ¬м „ мҡ”кө¬мӮ¬н•ӯ
```
- C++17 нҳёнҷҳ м»ҙнҢҢмқјлҹ¬ (GCC 7+, Clang 5+)
- LLVM 15.0+
- CMake 3.16+
- Git
```

### л№Ңл“ң (кө¬нҳ„ мҳҲм •)
```bash
git clone https://gogs.dclub.kr/kim/zlang.git
cd zlang-project
mkdir build && cd build
cmake ..
make
./zlang --version
```

### мІ« н”„лЎңк·ёлһЁ
```z-lang
// hello.z
fn main() {
    println("Hello, Z-Lang!");
}
```

```bash
./zlang hello.z
./hello
# м¶ңл Ҙ: Hello, Z-Lang!
```

---

## рҹ“ҡ л¬ём„ң

### н”„лЎңм қнҠё л¬ём„ң
| л¬ём„ң | м„ӨлӘ… |
|------|------|
| [PROJECT_PLAN.md](PROJECT_PLAN.md) | 8мЈј н•ҷмҠө кі„нҡҚ л°Ҹ м„ұкіө кё°мӨҖ |
| [LANGUAGE_SPECIFICATION.md](docs/LANGUAGE_SPECIFICATION.md) | мҷ„м „н•ң м–ём–ҙ лӘ…м„ё (v0.1) |
| [COMPILER_DESIGN.md](docs/COMPILER_DESIGN.md) | м»ҙнҢҢмқјлҹ¬ лӮҙл¶Җ кө¬мЎ° (к°ңл°ң мӨ‘) |
| [USER_GUIDE.md](docs/USER_GUIDE.md) | мӮ¬мҡ©мһҗ к°Җмқҙл“ң (к°ңл°ң мӨ‘) |
| [PERFORMANCE_ANALYSIS.md](docs/PERFORMANCE_ANALYSIS.md) | м„ұлҠҘ л¶„м„қ л°Ҹ лІӨм№ҳл§ҲнҒ¬ (к°ңл°ң мӨ‘) |

### LLVM к°•мқҳ мӢңлҰ¬мҰҲ (LLVM Legend Course)
| к°•мқҳ | кіјм ң | мғҒнғң |
|------|------|------|
| [1.1: LLVM м•„нӮӨн…ҚмІҳмҷҖ IR](docs/1_1_LLVM_ARCHITECTURE_AND_IR.md) | [ASSIGNMENT_1_1.md](ASSIGNMENT_1_1.md) | вң… мҷ„лЈҢ |
| [1.2: LLVM C API мҪ”л“ң мғқм„ұ](docs/1_2_LLVM_C_API_CODEGEN.md) | [ASSIGNMENT_1_2.md](ASSIGNMENT_1_2.md) | вң… мҷ„лЈҢ |
| [1.3: м ңм–ҙ нқҗлҰ„ (Control Flow)](docs/1_3_LLVM_CONTROL_FLOW.md) | [ASSIGNMENT_1_3.md](ASSIGNMENT_1_3.md) | вң… мҷ„лЈҢ |
| [1.4: нғҖмһ… мӢңмҠӨн…ңкіј ліөн•© кө¬мЎ°мІҙ](docs/1_4_LLVM_TYPE_SYSTEMS.md) | [ASSIGNMENT_1_4.md](ASSIGNMENT_1_4.md) | вң… мҷ„лЈҢ |
| [1.5: JIT м»ҙнҢҢмқјкіј мӢӨн–ү м—”м§„](docs/1_5_JIT_EXECUTION_ENGINE_PREVIEW.md) | мҳҲм • | рҹ”ң 2026-03-09 |

---

## рҹҸ—пёҸ н”„лЎңм қнҠё кө¬мЎ°

```
zlang-project/
в”ңв”Җв”Җ src/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ main.cpp                 # м§„мһ…м җ

в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ semantic/

в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ TypeChecker.cpp
в”Ӯ   в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ SymbolTable.h
в”Ӯ   в””в”Җв”Җ codegen/
в”Ӯ       в”ңв”Җв”Җ CodeGenerator.h
в”Ӯ       в”ңв”Җв”Җ CodeGenerator.cpp
в”Ӯ       в””в”Җв”Җ LLVMBackend.h
в”ңв”Җв”Җ docs/
в”Ӯ   в”ңв”Җв”Җ LANGUAGE_SPECIFICATION.md

в”ңв”Җв”Җ examples/
```

---

## рҹ“– м–ём–ҙ нҠ№м§•

### 1. Zero-Latency м„Өкі„

```z-lang
// мӢӨмӢңк°„ мӢңмҠӨн…ңм—җ мөңм Ғнҷ”
#[wcet_bound = "100_us"]  // WCET мһҗлҸҷ кІҖмҰқ
fn sensor_read() -> i32 {
    return read_adc(0);
}
```

### 2. Type Safety

```z-lang
// Rust мҠӨнғҖмқј Ownership
let x = Box::new(42);
let y = x;  // мҶҢмң к¶Ң мқҙлҸҷ
// xлҠ” лҚ” мқҙмғҒ мӮ¬мҡ© л¶Ҳк°Җ (м»ҙнҢҢмқј м—җлҹ¬)

// Lifetime м¶”м Ғ
fn borrow<'a>(x: &'a i32) -> &'a i32 {
    return x;
}
```

### 3. Real-Time Guarantees

```z-lang
// л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ н• лӢ№ м ңм•Ҫ
#[no_alloc]
fn hardreal_time() {
    let arr: [i32; 100];  // м •м Ғ н• лӢ№л§Ң OK
    // let vec = Vec::new();  // м»ҙнҢҢмқј м—җлҹ¬!
}
```

### 4. Multi-Platform Support

```z-lang
// нғҖкІҹ лӘ…мӢң
#[target = "x86-64-linux"]
#[target = "arm64-bare-metal"]
#[target = "riscv64-imac"]

fn main() { }
```

---

## рҹӣ пёҸ к°ңл°ң лЎңл“ңл§ө

### Week 1: LLVM кё°мҙҲ + м–ём–ҙ м„Өкі„
```
в”ңв”Җ LLVM C++ API н•ҷмҠө
в”ңв”Җ LLVM IR мқҙн•ҙ
в”ңв”Җ Z-Lang л¬ёлІ• м •мқҳ
в””в”Җ л үм„ң кё°ліё кө¬мЎ°
```

### Week 2: Lexer & Parser
```
в”ңв”Җ нҶ нҒ°нҷ” (Tokenization)
в”ңв”Җ кө¬л¬ё л¶„м„қ (Parsing)
в”ңв”Җ AST мғқм„ұ
в””в”Җ нҢҢм„ң кІҖмҰқ н…ҢмҠӨнҠё
```

### Week 3: мқҳлҜё л¶„м„қ
```
в”ңв”Җ нғҖмһ… кІҖмӮ¬ (Type Checking)
в”ңв”Җ мӢ¬ліј н…Ңмқҙлё” кҙҖлҰ¬
в”ңв”Җ м—җлҹ¬ мІҳлҰ¬
в””в”Җ мқҳлҜё л¶„м„қ н…ҢмҠӨнҠё
```

### Week 4: LLVM мҪ”л“ң мғқм„ұ
```
в”ңв”Җ AST вҶ’ LLVM IR ліҖнҷҳ
в”ңв”Җ н•ЁмҲҳ мғқм„ұ
в”ңв”Җ ліҖмҲҳ кҙҖлҰ¬
в””в”Җ мҪ”л“ң мғқм„ұ н…ҢмҠӨнҠё
```

### Week 5: Real-Time кё°лҠҘ
```
в”ңв”Җ WCET мһҗлҸҷ кі„мӮ°
в”ңв”Җ л©”лӘЁлҰ¬ м•Ҳм „м„ұ кІҖмҰқ
в”ңв”Җ Safety-Critical м§Җмӣҗ
в””в”Җ мӢӨмӢңк°„ кІҖмҰқ н…ҢмҠӨнҠё
```

### Week 6: н‘ңмӨҖ лқјмқҙлёҢлҹ¬лҰ¬
```
в”ңв”Җ I/O н•ЁмҲҳ (println, read, write)
в”ңв”Җ Math н•ЁмҲҳ (sqrt, sin, cos, ...)
в”ңв”Җ Collections (Vec, HashMap)
в””в”Җ лқјмқҙлёҢлҹ¬лҰ¬ н…ҢмҠӨнҠё
```

### Week 7: мөңм Ғнҷ” & м„ұлҠҘ
```
в”ңв”Җ LLVM Pass мһ‘м„ұ
в”ңв”Җ м„ұлҠҘ лІӨм№ҳл§ҲнӮ№
в”ңв”Җ н”„лЎңнҢҢмқјл§Ғ
в””в”Җ м„ұлҠҘ мөңм Ғнҷ”
```

### Week 8: л¬ём„ңнҷ” & мҷ„м„ұ
```
в”ңв”Җ мөңмў… л¬ём„ңнҷ”
в”ңв”Җ н•ҷмҲ  л…јл¬ё мһ‘м„ұ
в”ңв”Җ мҳӨн”ҲмҶҢмҠӨ мӨҖл№„
в””в”Җ мөңмў… л°ңн‘ң
```

---

## рҹ§Ә н…ҢмҠӨнҠё

## рҹ“Ҡ м„ұкіј кё°лҢҖ

### мҪ”л“ң нҶөкі„
```
мҙқ мӨ„ мҲҳ:       5,000~10,000 мӨ„ (C++)
кө¬нҳ„ нҢҢмқј:      20~30к°ң
```

message.content:
Your task is to create a detailed summary of the conversation so far, paying close attention to the user's explicit requests and your previous actions.
This summary should be thorough in capturing technical details, code patterns, and architectural decisions that would be essential for continuing development work without losing context.

Before providing your final summary, wrap your analysis in <analysis> tags to organize your thoughts and ensure you've covered all necessary points. In your analysis process:

1. Chronologically analyze each message and section of the conversation. For each section thoroughly identify:
   - The user's explicit requests and intents
   - Your approach to addressing the user's requests
   - Key decisions, technical concepts and code patterns
   - Specific details like:
     - file names
     - full code snippets
     - function signatures
     - file edits
  - Errors that you ran into and how you fixed them
  - Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
2. Double-check for technical accuracy and completeness, addressing each required element thoroughly.

Your summary should include the following sections:

1. Primary Request and Intent: Capture all of the user's explicit requests and intents in detail
2. Key Technical Concepts: List all important technical concepts, technologies, and frameworks discussed.
3. Files and Code Sections: Enumerate specific files and code sections examined, modified, or created. Pay special attention to the most recent messages and include full code snippets where applicable and include a summary of why this file read or edit is important.
4. Errors and fixes: List all errors that you ran into, and how you fixed them. Pay special attention to specific user feedback that you received, especially if the user told you to do something differently.
5. Problem Solving: Document problems solved and any ongoing troubleshooting efforts.
6. All user messages: List ALL user messages that are not tool results. These are critical for understanding the users' feedback and changing intent.
6. Pending Tasks: Outline any pending tasks that you have explicitly been asked to work on.
7. Current Work: Describe in detail precisely what was being worked on immediately before this summary request, paying special attention to the most recent messages from both user and assistant. Include file names and code snippets where applicable.
8. Optional Next Step: List the next step that you will take that is related to the most recent work you were doing. IMPORTANT: ensure that this step is DIRECTLY in line with the user's most recent explicit requests, and the task you were working on immediately before this summary request. If your last task was concluded, then only list next steps if they are explicitly in line with the users request. Do not start on tangential requests or really old requests that were already completed without confirming with the user first.
                       If there is a next step, include direct quotes from the most recent conversation showing exactly what task you were working on and where you left off. This should be verbatim to ensure there's no drift in task interpretation.

Here's an example of how your output should be structured:

<example>
<analysis>
[Your thought process, ensuring all points are covered thoroughly and accurately]
</analysis>

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   [Detailed description]

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - [Concept 1]
   - [Concept 2]
   - [...]

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - [File Name 1]
      - [Summary of why this file is important]
      - [Summary of the changes made to this file, if any]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [File Name 2]
      - [Important Code Snippet]
   - [...]

4. Errors and fixes:
    - [Detailed description of error 1]:
      - [How you fixed the error]
      - [User feedback on the error if any]
    - [...]

5. Problem Solving:
   [Description of solved problems and ongoing troubleshooting]

6. All user messages: 
    - [Detailed non tool use user message]
    - [...]

7. Pending Tasks:
   - [Task 1]
   - [Task 2]
   - [...]

8. Current Work:
   [Precise description of current work]

9. Optional Next Step:
   [Optional Next step to take]

</summary>
</example>

Please provide your summary based on the conversation so far, following this structure and ensuring precision and thoroughness in your response. 

There may be additional summarization instructions provided in the included context. If so, remember to follow these instructions when creating the above summary. Examples of instructions include:
<example>
## Compact Instructions
When summarizing the conversation focus on typescript code changes and also remember the mistakes you made and how you fixed them.
</example>

<example>
# Summary instructions
When you are using compact - please focus on test output and code changes. Include file reads verbatim.
</example>


IMPORTANT: Do NOT use any tools. You MUST respond with ONLY the <summary>...</summary> block as your text output.

2. **User's Main Requests Throughout**:

   - Create strong documentation for each learning phase

3. **Phases Completed**:
   - **LLVM 1.1**: LLVM Architecture and IR (lecture + assignment)
   - **LLVM 1.2**: LLVM C API Code Generation (lecture + assignment)
   - **LLVM 1.3**: Control Flow Design (lecture + assignment)
   - **LLVM 1.4**: Type Systems and Struct Design (lecture + assignment)
   - **LLVM 1.5**: JIT Compilation and Execution Engine (lecture + assignment)

5. **Key Technical Concepts Covered**:

content.text:
<analysis>
Looking at this conversation chronologically:

1. **Initial Phase (Messages 1-3)**: User transitioned from Zig OS studies to Z-Lang LLVM project. Explicitly requested creation of comprehensive LLVM learning curriculum with "м ҖмһҘ н•„мҲҳ л„ҲлҠ” кё°лЎқмқҙ мҰқлӘ…мқҙлӢӨ gogs" philosophy.

2. **LLVM 1.1-1.3 Phase**: User provided complete lecture content for stages 1.1 (Architecture), 1.2 (C API), and 1.3 (Control Flow). I created corresponding lecture markdown files, assignment files, and preview for next stage. All committed to gogs with specific commit messages documenting completions.

3. **LLVM 1.4-1.5 Phase**: User provided complete lecture content for 1.4 (Type Systems) and 1.5 (JIT Execution Engine). I created all corresponding materials and pushed to gogs (commit 4f6dd2b for 1.4).

4. **LLVM 2.1 Phase (Most Recent)**: User provided complete lecture content for 2.1 (Lexing/Tokenization) and explicitly requested I create all materials and save to gogs. This represents the transition from Backend (1.1-1.5) to Frontend (2.1+).

5. **Current State**: I just completed:
   - docs/2_1_LLVM_LEXING_TOKENIZATION.md (~4000 lines of detailed lecture)
   - ASSIGNMENT_2_1.md (~600 lines of detailed assignment tasks)
   - docs/2_2_LLVM_PARSING_AST_PREVIEW.md (preview for next stage)
   - README.md update showing Backend stage completion and Frontend stage 2.1 completion

6. **Pattern Recognition**: Each stage follows same structure:
   - Comprehensive lecture markdown (3000-4500 lines)
   - Detailed assignment with 4 tasks
   - Preview for next stage
   - README updates
   - Git commits and gogs pushes

7. **Technical Patterns**: All files maintain consistent structure: overview, multiple main sections with examples, code templates, detailed requirements, expected results, and next steps.

8. **User Feedback**: User consistently ends with explicit save request to gogs and "мӨҖл№„лҗҳм…ЁлӢӨл©ҙ 'лӢӨмқҢ'мқ„ мһ…л Ҙн•ҙ мЈјм„ёмҡ”!" (If ready, input 'next'!), indicating they want me to continue but are waiting for confirmation before providing next stage content.
</analysis>

<summary>
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   The user's primary intent is to create a comprehensive 8-week LLVM learning curriculum called "Z-Lang: LLVM Legend" - a new programming language project based on LLVM. The explicit requests are:
   - Create detailed lecture materials for each stage (1.1-1.8, then 2.1-2.8+)
   - Create assignment files with specific tasks for each stage
   - Save all materials to gogs repository (https://gogs.dclub.kr/kim/zlang.git)
   - Follow the philosophy "м ҖмһҘ н•„мҲҳ л„ҲлҠ” кё°лЎқмқҙ мҰқлӘ…мқҙлӢӨ gogs" (Save required, your record is your proof, gogs)
   - Document both theoretical understanding and practical implementation
   - Create preview/teaser files for upcoming stages to maintain momentum

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - LLVM IR (Intermediate Representation) - 3 forms: in-memory, bitcode (.bc), assembly (.ll)
   - SSA (Static Single Assignment) - each variable assigned exactly once
   - PHI nodes - control flow in SSA form
   - LLVM C API - Context, Module, Builder pattern
   - Basic Blocks and control flow graphs
   - GEP (GetElementPointer) - structure field access and address calculation
   - Memory layout, alignment, and padding
   - Function types and Calling Conventions
   - JIT (Just-In-Time) compilation vs AOT (Ahead-of-Time)
   - ExecutionEngine and MCJIT
   - Optimization Passes (PassManager)
   - Lexing (Lexical Analysis/Tokenization) - state machine based
   - Token types (Keyword, Identifier, Number, Operator, Punctuation)
   - Lookahead and character classification
   - Keyword tables and hash maps for optimization
   - Source location tracking for error messages
   - Recursive descent parsing (introduced in 2.2 preview)
   - AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) construction

3. Files and Code Sections:
   
   Backend Stage (1.1-1.5) - COMPLETED:
   
   - docs/1_1_LLVM_ARCHITECTURE_AND_IR.md
     - 2,500+ line comprehensive lecture on compiler architecture
     - Covers 3-stage compiler structure, SSA format, LLVM IR forms
     - Complete code examples showing IR generation
   
   - ASSIGNMENT_1_1.md
     - 4 tasks: IR analysis, register tracking, type mapping, efficiency analysis
     - Practical exercises on LLVM IR understanding
   
   - docs/1_2_LLVM_C_API_CODEGEN.md
     - 2,500+ line lecture on LLVM C API usage
     - 3 essential elements: Context, Module, Builder
     - 6-step function creation workflow with code examples
     - Complete Zig code template for add function
   
   - ASSIGNMENT_1_2.md
     - 5 tasks: environment setup, add function, mul function, IR output, challenges
     - build.zig configuration for LLVM linking
   
   - docs/1_3_LLVM_CONTROL_FLOW.md
     - 3,000+ line lecture on control flow implementation
     - If-Else 4-block structure (Entry, Then, Else, Merge)
     - PHI nodes and SSA principles
     - Loop 5-block structure (Preheader, Header, Body, Latch, Exit)
     - LLVM optimization techniques
   
   - ASSIGNMENT_1_3.md
     - 4 tasks: abs function, PHI analysis, loop implementation, SSA analysis
     - Complete IR examples for control flow
   
   - docs/1_4_LLVM_TYPE_SYSTEMS.md
     - 3,500+ line lecture on type systems and structures
     - Basic types (Integer, Float, Pointer, Array)
     - Struct design (Literal and Identified structs)
     - Memory alignment and padding
     - GEP instruction and address calculation
     - LLVM C API for type definition
   
   - ASSIGNMENT_1_4.md
     - 4 tasks: Point struct implementation, field access, array design, GEP analysis
     - Memory layout visualization exercises
   
   - docs/1_5_LLVM_JIT_EXECUTION_ENGINE.md
     - 3,500+ line lecture on JIT compilation and execution
     - ExecutionEngine architecture and responsibilities
     - MCJIT vs ORC JIT comparison
     - Native target initialization
     - Function pointer conversion and direct CPU execution
     - GC cooperation with Stack Maps
     - Optimization passes and PassManager integration
     - Performance measurement techniques
   
   - ASSIGNMENT_1_5.md
     - 4 tasks: native execution, pass comparison, function pointer analysis, AOT vs JIT comparison
     - Performance benchmarking requirements
   
   Frontend Stage (2.1+) - IN PROGRESS:
   
   - docs/2_1_LLVM_LEXING_TOKENIZATION.md (JUST CREATED)
     - 4,000+ line comprehensive lecture on lexical analysis
     - Token definition and types (Keyword, Identifier, Number, String, Operator, Punctuation)
     - Lexer algorithm based on state machines
     - Lookahead and character classification
     - Keyword table optimization using Hash Maps vs Trie
     - Source location tracking (line, column) for error messages
     - Special case handling (strings, comments, numbers with decimals)
     - Complete Lexer struct and implementation patterns
     - Separation of Lexer and Parser design benefits
     - Key code pattern:
       ```zig
       pub const TokenType = enum {
           number, identifier, string,
           keyword_fn, keyword_let, keyword_const,
           // ... more tokens
           eof
       };

       pub const Token = struct {
           tag: TokenType,
           lexeme: []const u8,
           line: u32,
           column: u32,
           literal: ?[]const u8 = null,
       };

       pub const Lexer = struct {
           source: []const u8,
           current: usize = 0,
           line: u32 = 1,
           column: u32 = 1,
           tokens: std.ArrayList(Token),

           pub fn tokenize(self: *Lexer) ![]Token { ... }
           fn advance(self: *Lexer) u8 { ... }
           fn peek(self: *const Lexer) u8 { ... }
       };
       ```
   
   - ASSIGNMENT_2_1.md (JUST CREATED)
     - 4 tasks:
       - Task 1: Token definition (10+ token types with enum)
       - Task 2: Lexer implementation for "fn add(a, b) { return a + b; }"
       - Task 3: Number processing (integers and decimals/floats)
       - Task 4: Structural analysis - why separate Lexer and Parser? (3-4 page document)
     - Expected output shows tokenization of function definition
     - Complete Lexer struct template provided
   
   - docs/2_2_LLVM_PARSING_AST_PREVIEW.md (JUST CREATED)
     - 1,500+ line preview of parsing stage
     - Token to AST conversion
     - Recursive descent parser introduction
     - AST node definition patterns
     - Operator precedence handling
     - Parser algorithm types (Recursive Descent, LL(1), LR)
     - Next phase roadmap showing connection to IR generation
   
   - README.md (UPDATED)
